docs.title: light-ml-platform
docs.text: A two-module portfolio project bridging platform engineering with ML infrastructure.

docs.title: Modules
docs.text:
• :material-console-line: recipes

    A lightweight CLI that converts simple YAML specs into Terraform configurations for AWS resources. Open-source and cloud-agnostic.

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: Get started

  • :material-pipe: kitchen

    A reusable MLOps framework for Kaggle and modeling projects — data I/O, experiment tracking, model serving, drift monitoring, and orchestration.

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: Get started

docs.title: Stack
docs.text:
Concern Tool IaC generation recipes CLI + Terraform Data versioning DVC + S3 Experiment tracking MLflow Model serving FastAPI + Docker → Lambda (ECR) Monitoring Evidently AI Orchestration Prefect

AWS-native where necessary; open-source tooling throughout.

docs.title: kitchen
docs.text: kitchen is a reusable MLOps framework for Kaggle and modeling projects. It covers the full model lifecycle: data versioning, experiment tracking, model serving, drift monitoring, and orchestration.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Kaggle data
    │
    ▼
[DVC] ingest ──► features ──► train ──► evaluate
                                  │
                             [MLflow] experiment tracking
                                  │
                             [Prefect] orchestration
                                  │
                             [FastAPI + Docker]
                                  │
                             Lambda (ECR)
                                  │
                             [Evidently] drift monitoring

docs.title: Stack
docs.text: Concern Tool Why Data versioning DVC + S3 Git-native, file-level, ML-standard Experiment tracking MLflow Open-source, self-hostable, AWS-native Serving FastAPI + Docker → Lambda Portable containers, serverless deploy Monitoring Evidently AI ML-specific drift detection for tabular data Orchestration Prefect Python-native, lightweight, modern DX

docs.title: Dataset
docs.text: kitchen supports any tabular Kaggle competition — the showcase projects use healthcare and sci-fi datasets.

docs.title: Data Versioning
docs.text: Data versioning is handled by DVC with an S3 backend. Raw and processed datasets are tracked outside Git but tied to specific commits via .dvc pointer files.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
1. Raw data is downloaded to data/raw/ during the ingest stage
  2. DVC tracks the data directory and stores it in S3
  3. .dvc pointer files are committed to Git — collaborators run dvc pull to fetch the actual data

docs.title: Pipeline stages
docs.text:
ingest → features → train → evaluate

Defined in dvc.yaml. Parameters are in params.yaml and tracked by DVC so changing a hyperparameter invalidates the downstream cache automatically.

docs.title: DVC remote setup
docs.text:
dvc remote add -d myremote s3://your-bucket/dvc-store
dvc remote modify myremote region us-east-1

docs.title: Common commands
docs.text:
dvc repro          # Run the full pipeline (skips cached stages)
dvc repro train    # Run from a specific stage
dvc push           # Push data artifacts to S3
dvc pull           # Pull data artifacts from S3
dvc params diff    # Show changed parameters vs last run
dvc metrics show   # Show tracked metrics

docs.title: params.yaml reference
docs.text:
data:
  source: kaggle
  dataset: heart-disease-uci
  target_col: target
  test_size: 0.2
  random_seed: 42

docs.title: Experiment Tracking
docs.text: Experiments are tracked with MLflow. Every training run logs parameters, metrics, and model artifacts automatically through kitchen.tracking.

docs.title: What gets tracked
docs.text: Item Example Parameters n_estimators, max_depth, learning_rate Metrics val_accuracy, val_brier, val_log_loss Artifacts Trained model (sklearn/xgboost/pyfunc flavour) Tags model_variant (baseline or challenger)

docs.title: Backend
docs.text:
The default backend is a local SQLite store — no server required for development:

# Set in .env (generated by kitchen init)
MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI=sqlite:///mlruns.db

For a shared remote server, point MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI at any MLflow-compatible store (PostgreSQL, MySQL, remote SQLite).

docs.title: View the UI
docs.text:
mlflow ui --backend-store-uri sqlite:///mlruns.db
# Open http://localhost:5000

docs.title: Using the Tracker
docs.text:
kitchen.tracking.Tracker wraps mlflow with param flattening and model logging:

from kitchen.tracking import Tracker, configure_from_env

configure_from_env()
tracker = Tracker("my-experiment")

with tracker.run(run_name="baseline", params=params) as run:
    import mlflow
    mlflow.set_tag("model_variant", "baseline")
    model = train(...)
    tracker.log_metrics({"val_accuracy": 0.79})
    tracker.log_model(model, "model", flavour="xgboost")

docs.title: Model registry and promotion
docs.text:
Models are registered and promoted via kitchen.registry:

python flows/promote.py --dry-run   # compare baseline vs challenger
python flows/promote.py             # register winner, set champion alias

The champion model is loaded with:

mlflow.sklearn.load_model("models:/my-competition-model@champion")

Promotion uses the champion alias rather than deprecated MLflow stages.

docs.title: Model Serving
docs.text: The trained model is served via a FastAPI application, packaged as a Docker container and deployed to AWS Lambda via ECR.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
ECR image → Lambda function URL
                │
         FastAPI + Mangum
                │
         /predict endpoint

Mangum adapts the FastAPI ASGI app to the Lambda event/response format.

docs.title: GET /health
docs.text:
Health check.

{"status": "ok"}

docs.title: POST /predict
docs.text:
Run inference on a single observation.

// Request
{
  "age": 63,
  "sex": 1,
  "cp": 3,
  ...
}

// Response
{
  "prediction": 1,
  "probability": 0.87
}

docs.title: Local development
docs.text:
cd src/serve
uvicorn app:app --reload

docs.title: Docker build
docs.text:
docker build -t my-project-serve ./src/serve
docker run -p 9000:8080 my-project-serve

docs.title: Deploy to Lambda
docs.text:
Tip

Use recipes to provision the Lambda function and ECR repo — see the recipes quickstart.

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text: Model and data health are monitored with Evidently AI. Reports are generated on a schedule and stored in monitoring/.

docs.title: What gets monitored
docs.text: Report Description Data drift Distribution shift between training reference and live data Data quality Missing values, out-of-range values, schema changes Model performance Accuracy, AUC degradation over time (requires ground truth labels)

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
The monitor_flow.py Prefect flow:

  1. Loads the training dataset as the reference distribution
  2. Loads the most recent batch of inference inputs as current data
  3. Runs Evidently drift and quality reports
  4. Saves HTML reports to monitoring/ and optionally uploads to S3

docs.title: Running manually
docs.text: python -m kitchen.flows.monitor_flow

docs.title: Report output
docs.text:
Reports are saved to monitoring/ as HTML files:

monitoring/
├── data_drift_2024-01-15.html
└── data_quality_2024-01-15.html

docs.title: Orchestration
docs.text: Pipeline runs are orchestrated with Prefect. Each competition project gets two experiment flows plus a generic single-run flow, all scaffolded by kitchen init.

docs.title: experiments/baseline.py
docs.text:
Runs the full training flow tagged as the baseline approach:

build_features → train_model → evaluate_model

Tags the MLflow run with model_variant=baseline.

python experiments/baseline.py

docs.title: experiments/challenger.py
docs.text:
Same structure as baseline but tagged model_variant=challenger. Edit this file to override params or add features before calling run_variant.

python experiments/challenger.py

docs.title: flows/train_flow.py
docs.text:
Generic single-run training pipeline from kitchen.flows.train_flow. Useful for quick iteration without experiment tagging.

python flows/train_flow.py

docs.title: flows/promote.py
docs.text:
Compares baseline vs challenger by metric and promotes the winner to the champion alias in the MLflow Model Registry.

python flows/promote.py --dry-run   # compare without promoting
python flows/promote.py             # promote best model

docs.title: kitchen/flows/monitor_flow.py
docs.text:
Drift detection flow — generates Evidently reports and uploads to S3:

load_reference_data → load_current_data → run_drift_report → upload_report

python -m kitchen.flows.monitor_flow

docs.title: Relationship to DVC
docs.text:
DVC and Prefect serve different purposes and complement each other:

Concern Tool Stage caching and data lineage DVC Experiment scheduling, retries, observability Prefect

Projects that want DVC-tracked pipelines can add a dvc.yaml alongside the Prefect flows — the kitchen DataStore paths (data/raw/, data/processed/) align with DVC stage conventions.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.11+
  • AWS CLI configured (aws configure)
  • Docker (for model serving)

docs.title: Start a new competition project
docs.text:
pip install -e path/to/light-ml-platform/kitchen
kitchen init my-competition
cd my-competition
pip install -e ../light-ml-platform/kitchen -e .
cp .env.example .env

docs.title: Download competition data
docs.text:
# Kaggle competitions
kaggle competitions download -c <competition-slug> -p data/raw/
unzip data/raw/<competition-slug>.zip -d data/raw/

docs.title: Implement the three required files
docs.text: File What to implement src/features/run.py build(raw_df) -> df — feature engineering src/train/run.py fit(df, params) -> model — model training src/evaluate/run.py evaluate(model, df) -> dict — metrics

docs.title: Run experiments
docs.text:
# Baseline approach
python experiments/baseline.py

# Challenger (after editing experiments/challenger.py)
python experiments/challenger.py

# Compare and promote best model
python flows/promote.py --dry-run
python flows/promote.py

# View MLflow UI
mlflow ui --backend-store-uri sqlite:///mlruns.db
# Open http://localhost:5000

docs.title: Generate a Kaggle submission
docs.text: python flows/generate_submission.py

docs.title: recipes
docs.text: recipes is a lightweight CLI that converts a simple YAML spec into Terraform configurations for AWS resources.

docs.title: Why recipes?
docs.text:
Writing Terraform for common AWS resources — Lambda functions, S3 buckets, IAM roles — involves a lot of repetitive boilerplate. recipes lets you declare what you want in a concise YAML spec and generates the correct, opinionated HCL for you.

recipes generate infra.yaml --out ./tf

docs.title: Design
docs.text:
• Input: a single YAML file describing your resources
  • Output: one .tf file per resource + a provider.tf
  • Extensible: adding a new resource type means adding a Pydantic model, a Jinja2 template, and a generator — nothing else changes

docs.title: Supported resources
docs.text: Type Description s3 S3 bucket with optional versioning iam_role IAM role with assume-role policy and managed policy attachments lambda Lambda function — image (ECR) or zip deployment

docs.title: Commands
docs.text: Command Description recipes generate SPEC Generate Terraform configs from a spec recipes validate SPEC Validate a spec without generating files

docs.title: CI/CD Integration
docs.text: recipes integrates with GitHub Actions to validate specs on every push and generate Terraform on merge to main.

docs.title: Workflow overview
docs.text:
Coming soon

This section will be completed once the CI/CD workflow is finalized. See .github/workflows/recipes-ci.yml for the current state.

docs.title: Validate on pull request
docs.text:
# .github/workflows/recipes-ci.yml
- name: Validate spec
  run: recipes validate infra.yaml

docs.title: Secret management
docs.text: Terraform state and AWS credentials should be stored as GitHub Actions secrets.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/rkoren/light-ml-platform.git
cd light-ml-platform/recipes
pip install -e .

docs.title: Your first spec
docs.text:
Create infra.yaml:

name: my-api
region: us-east-1

resources:
  - type: s3
    name: my-api-artifacts
    versioning: true

  - type: iam_role
    name: my-api-exec
    service: lambda.amazonaws.com
    policies:
      - arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole

  - type: lambda
    name: my-api
    role: my-api-exec
    image_uri: "123456789.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/my-api:latest"
    memory: 512
    timeout: 30

docs.title: Generate
docs.text:
recipes generate infra.yaml --out ./tf

Output:

  ✓ provider.tf
  ✓ my-api-artifacts.tf (s3)
  ✓ my-api-exec.tf (iam_role)
  ✓ my-api.tf (lambda)

Generated 4 file(s) → tf/

docs.title: Validate without generating
docs.text:
recipes validate infra.yaml
# ✓ spec is valid

docs.title: Apply with Terraform
docs.text:
cd tf
terraform init
terraform plan
terraform apply

Tip

The generated configs are plain HCL — you can edit them after generation. recipes is a starting point, not a wrapper.

docs.title: YAML Spec Reference
docs.text: Every spec file has a root object with metadata and a list of resources.

docs.title: Root fields
docs.text: Field Type Required Default Description name string yes — Logical name for this spec region string no us-east-1 AWS region for the provider block resources list no [] List of resource definitions

docs.title: s3
docs.text:
Provisions an S3 bucket with optional versioning.

- type: s3
  name: my-bucket
  versioning: true
Field Type Required Default name string yes — versioning bool no false

docs.title: iam_role
docs.text:
Provisions an IAM role with an assume-role policy and optional managed policy attachments.

- type: iam_role
  name: my-exec-role
  service: lambda.amazonaws.com
  policies:
    - arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole
Field Type Required Default name string yes — service string yes — policies list[string] no []

docs.title: lambda
docs.text:
Provisions a Lambda function. Supports both image-based (ECR) and zip-based deployment.

# Image-based (ECR)
- type: lambda
  name: my-function
  role: my-exec-role
  image_uri: "123456789.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/my-fn:latest"
  memory: 512
  timeout: 30
  environment:
    TABLE_NAME: my-table

# Zip-based
- type: lambda
  name: my-function
  role: my-exec-role
  runtime: python3.11
  handler: src.main.handler
  memory: 128
  timeout: 3
Field Type Required Default name string yes — role string yes — image_uri string no null runtime string no null handler string no null memory int no 128 timeout int no 3 environment dict no {}

Note

Set either image_uri (for container image deployment) or runtime + handler (for zip deployment), not both.
